title: Equivalent Expressions: Graphing and Slider Methods in Desmos
description: A from-scratch lesson on equivalent-expression questions — what equivalence means, why graphing and sliders work, and how to use each, one idea at a time.

blocks.html:
Equivalent Expressions

In this lesson you'll learn a fast, reliable way to answer "which expression is equivalent?" questions using Desmos.

We'll start from the very beginning — what "equivalent" even means — and build up from there, one step at a time.

What does "equivalent" mean?

Two expressions are equivalent if they always give the same value — no matter what number you plug in for the variable.

A quick example

Are \(2(x+3)\) and \(2x+6\) equivalent?

Try \(x=5\):   \(2(5+3)=16\)  and  \(2(5)+6=16\). ✓

Try \(x=0\):   \(2(0+3)=6\)  and  \(2(0)+6=6\). ✓

They match for every input, so yes — they're equivalent. They're just two ways of writing the same thing.

choices: Yes, they're equivalent
No, they're not
content.hint: Pick a number for \(x\) and work out each expression. Do they land on the same value?
content.explanation: Right — they're not equivalent. \(x+x = 2x\), which isn't the same as \(x^2\). Try \(x=3\): \(x+x=6\) but \(x^2=9\). Differing for even one input is enough.

blocks.html:
What these questions look like

On the SAT, an equivalent-expressions question gives you one expression and four answer choices. Your job is to pick the choice that's equivalent to the original.

Here's a real one

Which expression is equivalent to \(\dfrac{4}{4x-5}-\dfrac{1}{x+1}\)?

A)  \(\dfrac{1}{(x+1)(4x-5)}\)

B)  \(\dfrac{3}{3x-6}\)

C)  \(-\dfrac{1}{(x+1)(4x-5)}\)

D)  \(\dfrac{9}{(x+1)(4x-5)}\)

You could combine the fractions by hand — but there's a faster way when you have answer choices to lean on.

Two Desmos shortcuts

When a question gives you answer choices, you can let Desmos do the checking for you:

  • Graphing — for expressions with one variable.
  • Sliders — for expressions with two or more variables.

Let's learn graphing first, since our example has just one variable, \(x\).

Why graphing works

A graph is simply a picture of an expression's value at every input. Each point shows one input (left–right) paired with its output (up–down).

So if two expressions are equivalent, they have the same output at every input — which means their graphs are exactly the same curve.

That's the whole idea: graph the original, graph a choice, and check whether they land right on top of each other.

content.prompt: If two expressions are equivalent, what do their graphs look like?
choices: Exactly the same curve
A similar shape, just shifted over
Two completely unrelated curves
content.hint: If two expressions always give the same output, what must be true about every point on their graphs?
content.explanation: Exactly — same output at every input means the graphs sit perfectly on top of each other, as one identical curve. That overlap is what you'll look for.

blocks.html:
The steps

  1. Graph the original expression.
  2. Graph one answer choice.
  3. If the two graphs overlap perfectly, that choice is equivalent. If not, move on to the next choice.

One small thing about \(x\)

Desmos graphs using the letter \(x\). If a question uses a different letter — like \(t\) or \(n\) — just rewrite it with \(x\); the name of the variable doesn't change anything. (Our example already uses \(x\), so we're ready to go.)

content.prompt: A question is written with the variable \(t\) instead of \(x\). To graph it, you should…
choices: Give up — Desmos only understands \(x\)
Rewrite every \(t\) as \(x\), then graph normally
Graph \(t\) on the up–down axis
content.hint: The variable's name is just a label. Does renaming it change which expressions are equal?
content.explanation: Correct — the letter is just a placeholder. Replace every \(t\) with \(x\) (in the original and the choices) and graph as usual.

blocks.html:
Let's test choice D

Back to our question. We'll check choice D, \(\dfrac{9}{(x+1)(4x-5)}\), against the original \(\dfrac{4}{4x-5}-\dfrac{1}{x+1}\). Graph both and watch for an overlap.

blocks.prompt_html: Follow the steps to enter the original and choice D, then compare the two graphs.
blocks.correct_html: Yes. The two graphs sit perfectly on top of each other, so choice D is equivalent to the original. That's the answer.
blocks.incorrect_html: Look once more — every point of choice D's curve lands on the original, including the breaks near \(x=-1\) and \(x=\tfrac{5}{4}\). A complete overlap like this means the two are equivalent.

blocks.html:
Choice D's graph lands exactly on the original, so D is the answer.

The other three choices would each draw a different curve that doesn't overlap. On the test, you'd try the choices one at a time until one matches — then stop.

When graphing won't work: more than one variable

A graph shows one input against one output. If an expression has two variables, there's no single curve to compare — so graphing can't help here. (We'll handle that case with sliders, coming up.)

content.prompt: You need an expression equivalent to \(5a^2b - 10ab^2\), which has two variables, \(a\) and \(b\). Is graph-and-match a good fit?
choices: Yes — graph it like any other expression
No — with two variables there's no single curve to compare, so use sliders
content.hint: Count the variables. Can a single curve on the \(xy\)-plane show two of them at once?
content.explanation: Correct — two variables means there's no single \(x\)-vs-\(y\) curve to overlay. That's exactly what the slider method is for.

blocks.html:
When graphing won't work: no answer choices

This method works by matching the original to a choice. If a question has no answer choices — you just type in your own answer — there's nothing to match against, so you'll need to do the algebra instead.

content.prompt: A grid-in question (no answer choices) asks you to write \(\dfrac{x^2-9}{x+3}\) in simplest form. Should you graph and match?
choices: Yes — graph it and read the answer off the screen
No — with nothing to compare against, simplify with algebra
content.hint: The method works by matching the original against a choice. Is there anything here to match against?
content.explanation: Correct — matching needs something to match against. With no choices, simplify by hand; here it's \(x-3\).

blocks.html:
The slider method

Now for expressions with two or more variables — like \(6x^8y^2+12x^2y^2\). Graphing can't draw a single curve for these, so we need a different idea.

Why sliders work

Go back to what "equivalent" means: the two expressions give the same value for any numbers you plug in. So instead of graphing, we can just pick values for the variables and compare the results.
A slider in Desmos is a little dial that sets a variable to a number you choose. Put the original and a choice on screen with the same slider values: if they show the same number, they're equivalent.

content.prompt: Two expressions are equivalent. You plug the same numbers into both. The results will be…
choices: The same number
Different numbers
Impossible to predict
content.hint: Go back to the definition of equivalent: same value for any inputs. So what happens with the same inputs?
content.explanation: Correct — equivalent means the same value for any inputs, so feeding both the same numbers gives the same result. That's exactly what sliders let you check.

blocks.html:
Our example

Which expression is equivalent to \(6x^8y^2+12x^2y^2\)?

A)  \(6x^2y^2(2x^6)\)

B)  \(6x^2y^2(x^4)\)

C)  \(6x^2y^2(x^6+2)\)

D)  \(6x^2y^2(x^4+2)\)

Two variables here (\(x\) and \(y\)), so this is a job for sliders.

One catch: \(x\) and \(y\) can't be sliders

Desmos reserves \(x\) and \(y\) for the graphing axes, so it won't turn them into sliders. The fix is to rename them to capital \(X\) and \(Y\). Rewrite the original and every choice the same way — for instance, the original becomes \(6X^8Y^2+12X^2Y^2\).

content.prompt: A multi-variable question is written with \(x\) and \(y\). To use sliders, what's the first thing you do?
choices: Nothing — Desmos makes \(x\) and \(y\) sliders like any letter
Rename \(x\) and \(y\) to capital \(X\) and \(Y\) everywhere
Switch back to the graphing method
content.hint: Desmos reserves \(x\) and \(y\) for its axes. Will it turn those particular letters into sliders?
content.explanation: Correct — Desmos keeps \(x\) and \(y\) for the axes, so rename them to \(X\) and \(Y\) (in the original and every choice) and the sliders appear.

blocks.html:
Set it up

Enter the original \(6X^8Y^2+12X^2Y^2\) and let Desmos create an \(X\) slider and a \(Y\) slider.

blocks.prompt_html: Enter the original expression with capital \(X\) and \(Y\), then create a slider for each.

blocks.html:
Now type each choice (rewritten with \(X\) and \(Y\)) on its own line and compare the values:

A)  \(6X^2Y^2(2X^6)\)

B)  \(6X^2Y^2(X^4)\)

C)  \(6X^2Y^2(X^6+2)\)

D)  \(6X^2Y^2(X^4+2)\)

Only choice C shows the same value as the original. C is the answer.

Important: move the sliders

If every slider sits at its starting value of \(1\), a wrong choice can match the original by luck. To avoid that, drag each slider to a different, random value — say \(X=3\) and \(Y=-2\). A choice that still matches at messy, unequal values is genuinely equivalent.

content.prompt: Why move the sliders off \(1\), and to different values from each other?
choices: Desmos can't compute when a slider is at \(1\)
At \(1\) (or equal values), a wrong choice can match by coincidence — varied values expose it
It makes the numbers smaller and easier to read
content.hint: Imagine every variable sitting at \(1\). Could a wrong choice land on the same number just by luck?
content.explanation: Correct — at \(1\) (or equal values) a wrong choice can match by coincidence. Distinct, non-\(1\) values break those accidental matches, so only a truly equivalent choice keeps up.

blocks.html:
Same limitation as before

Just like graphing, the slider method only works when there are answer choices to compare against. No choices? Fall back on algebra.

content.prompt: The slider method shares one big limitation with the graphing method. Which is it?
choices: It only works when there are exactly two variables
It needs answer choices to compare against
It only works for polynomials
content.hint: Both Desmos methods work by comparing the original to something. What do they need in order to compare?
content.explanation: Correct — both Desmos methods are matching tools. No answer choices means nothing to match, so you'd use algebra instead.

blocks.html:
Putting it together

A quick guide for the next equivalent-expressions question you see:

  • One variable + answer choices? Graph and match. Rename the variable to \(x\) if needed.
  • Two or more variables + answer choices? Use sliders. Rename \(x,y\) to \(X,Y\), and move every slider to a different, non-\(1\) value.
  • No answer choices? Neither shortcut applies — there's nothing to match, so solve it with algebra.

Match the tool to what's in front of you, and these questions become quick and almost mistake-proof.
